Selling on Amazon China or Tmall? You Need a Trademark First

Tmall, JD.com, Douyin Shop, and Amazon all require a registered Chinese trademark. Learn the platform requirements and why you should file now.

Key Takeaway

If you want to sell on China's major e-commerce platforms — Tmall, JD.com, Douyin Shop, or even Amazon's China-facing operations — a registered trademark in China is mandatory. No trademark, no storefront. This isn't just a recommendation; it's a platform requirement. Getting your trademark registered before applying for marketplace access can save you months of delays.

The E-Commerce Gateway to China

China's e-commerce market is the largest in the world, with over 1 trillion USD in annual online sales. For international brands, platforms like Tmall, JD.com, and Douyin Shop (formerly TikTok Shop) represent the fastest path to Chinese consumers.

But there's a catch: every major Chinese e-commerce platform requires a registered Chinese trademark before you can open an official store. This isn't optional — it's a hard requirement built into the platform onboarding process.

If you're planning to sell in China through e-commerce, your trademark application needs to happen before your marketplace application. The registration process takes 9-12 months, so plan accordingly.

Platform-by-Platform Requirements

Tmall (天猫)

Tmall is China's premium B2C platform, owned by Alibaba. Requirements include:

  • Registered Chinese trademark (R-mark, not just TM application)
  • Brand must be registered in the relevant product class
  • Company registration documents
  • Brand authorization letter (if you're a distributor)

Tmall's review process is strict. They verify trademark authenticity, check for conflicts, and may reject applications if the brand doesn't meet quality standards.

JD.com (京东)

JD.com is China's second-largest e-commerce platform. Their requirements are similar:

  • Registered trademark in China
  • Business license
  • Product quality certificates
  • Brand authorization

JD.com tends to be slightly more flexible on brand requirements for certain categories, but the trademark requirement is non-negotiable.

Douyin Shop (抖音小店)

The fastest-growing platform in China, powered by TikTok's parent company ByteDance:

  • Registered trademark required for brand stores
  • Individual sellers may use TM (pending) applications for some categories
  • Brand authorization chain for distributors

Amazon China

While Amazon's direct China operations have scaled back, Amazon Global Selling (cross-border e-commerce) still serves Chinese consumers:

  • Brand Registry requires a registered trademark
  • Trademark must be in the country where you're registering
  • For China operations specifically, a CNIPA-registered mark is required

Why Platforms Require Trademarks

Consumer Protection

China's e-commerce platforms are legally responsible for the products sold on their platforms. Requiring trademarks helps them:

  • Verify brand authenticity
  • Prevent counterfeit goods
  • Resolve disputes with clear ownership records
  • Comply with China's consumer protection laws

Brand Ecosystem

Platforms like Tmall have invested heavily in premium brand experiences. Trademark requirements ensure that only legitimate brands access these premium placements, protecting both brands and consumers.

Platform Liability

Under Chinese law, platforms can be held liable for trademark infringement occurring on their sites. Requiring registered trademarks shifts some of this risk to sellers.

The Timeline Problem

Trademark Registration: 9-12 Months

The CNIPA trademark registration process takes approximately 9-12 months from application to certificate. This includes:

  • Filing and formal examination (1-3 months)
  • Substantive examination (6-9 months)
  • Publication and opposition period (3 months)
  • Certificate issuance (1-2 weeks)

Platform Application: 1-3 Months

After receiving your trademark certificate, the platform application and review process takes an additional 1-3 months.

Total Timeline: 10-15 Months

From trademark filing to live storefront, expect 10-15 months minimum. If you haven't started the trademark process, start now — even before you've finalized your marketplace strategy.

Strategy: File Your Trademark Now

Don't Wait for Perfect Timing

Many brands delay trademark filing until they're "ready" to enter China. By then, they've already lost 9-12 months. File your trademark application as soon as you begin considering China e-commerce — you can always decide not to enter, but you can't speed up the registration timeline.

File in the Right Classes

E-commerce sellers typically need:

  • Product class: The Nice Classification class for your specific products
  • Class 35: Advertising and retail services (required by many platforms for storefronts)
  • Class 9: If you have a mobile app or software component

Register Both English and Chinese Names

Chinese consumers search in Chinese. If your brand only has an English trademark, you're invisible to a large portion of the market. Register your Chinese brand name separately.

How RTMCN Can Help

At RTMCN, we help international e-commerce sellers navigate China's trademark and marketplace requirements. Our services include:

  • Fast-track trademark filing: We prepare and submit applications within 48 hours
  • Platform-compliant class selection: We identify the exact classes platforms require
  • Chinese name advisory: We develop and register your Chinese brand name
  • Brand registry support: We provide documentation for Tmall, JD.com, and other platform applications
  • Ongoing monitoring: We watch for conflicting filings that could affect your platform presence

Next Steps

Every month you delay trademark filing is a month you can't sell on Chinese platforms. Start the process today.

Action items:

  1. Start your trademark application — We'll file within 48 hours of receiving your information
  2. Check your product classes — Identify which classes you need for your specific products
  3. Read our complete guide — Understand the full timeline from filing to certificate
